title: Topic 227: The Psychology of Game Music: How Sound Shapes Experience
content:
# Topic 227: The Psychology of Game Music: How Sound Shapes Experience

## ABSTRACT

This paper bravely ventures into the cacophonous world of video game music, a realm where beeps and boops have evolved into complex orchestral scores designed to manipulate your very soul. We explore the psychological wizardry that composers and sound designers employ to make you feel, remember, and, most importantly, keep playing. From the Pavlovian response to a 1-UP sound to the wallet-loosening power of a triumphant loot box jingle, we dissect how game music masterfully transforms grown adults into button-mashing, teary-eyed, or free-spending zombies. This investigation will forever change the way you listen to game soundtracks, revealing them not as mere background noise, but as the puppet strings controlling your every in-game move. You have been warned.

## INTRODUCTION

Remember the first time you heard the overworld theme from *Super Mario Bros.*? That cheerful, calypso-infused melody is more than just a nostalgic earworm; it’s a masterclass in psychological manipulation. It’s the auditory equivalent of a reassuring pat on the back, a sonic promise that everything is going to be okay, even when you’re a portly plumber in a world filled with homicidal turtles and sentient mushrooms. This, in a nutshell, is the power of game music. It’s a subtle force, a puppet master pulling at our emotional strings, and we, the players, are its willing marionettes.

For decades, video game music was the underdog of the gaming world, a tinny, 8-bit symphony often dismissed as a childish cacophony. But as technology has evolved, so too has the sophistication of game audio. Today’s soundtracks are often epic, orchestral productions that rival Hollywood film scores. But their purpose remains the same: to immerse us, to guide us, and to subtly influence our behavior. This paper will explore the fascinating psychology behind game music, from its ability to evoke powerful emotions to its role in shaping our in-game decisions and even our real-world spending habits. We will delve into the academic research, the real-world examples, and the witty observations that reveal the true power of the beep, the boop, and the boom. So, plug in your headphones, turn up the volume, and prepare to have your mind blown. Or, at the very least, to finally understand why you can’t get the *Tetris* theme out of your head.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

Academics, in their infinite wisdom, have long been fascinated by the power of music. They’ve published countless papers on how music affects our moods, our memories, and even our perception of time. But when it comes to the specific realm of video game music, the research is still a bit like a side quest in a massive open-world RPG: intriguing, but not yet fully explored. Nevertheless, the existing literature provides a solid foundation for understanding the psychological impact of game audio.

One of the most cited concepts is the theory of “immersion,” the feeling of being completely absorbed in the game world. Researchers like Evans (2018) have found that music is a key ingredient in this immersive stew, helping to “envelop players in the game space.” Of course, anyone who’s ever jumped out of their skin at the sound of a zombie’s groan in *Resident Evil* could have told you that. But it’s nice to have it confirmed by science.

Then there’s the emotional component. Studies by Klimmt et al. (2019) have shown that soundtrack music can intensify our emotional responses to games. They found that the music in *Assassin’s Creed: Black Flag* made players feel more positive emotions, while the score of *Alien: Isolation* amplified their feelings of horror. This is hardly surprising. After all, it’s the ominous music that tells us something bad is about to happen, long before the xenomorph appears on screen. It’s the auditory equivalent of a giant, flashing “DANGER” sign.

But perhaps the most interesting area of research is the connection between game music and memory. A study by Belfi, Karlan, and Tranel (2016) found that music evokes more vivid autobiographical memories than even images of famous faces. This explains why hearing a snippet of a beloved game’s soundtrack can instantly transport us back to our childhood bedrooms, a controller in hand and a smile on our face. It’s a powerful form of nostalgia, and game developers are all too aware of its potency.

Of course, not all research is so high-minded. Some studies have delved into the nitty-gritty of how sound can influence our behavior. Research on gambling has shown that fast-paced music can lead to riskier betting, while the celebratory sounds of a win, even a small one, can encourage players to keep going. This is the dark side of game audio, the point where it crosses the line from entertainment to manipulation. And it’s a topic we’ll be exploring in more detail later in this paper.

## ANALYSIS

The psychological impact of game music can be broken down into a few key concepts, each more cunning than the last. First, there’s the idea of “cognitive flow,” a state of complete immersion where the player is fully absorbed in the game. As described by Csikszentmihalyi, this is the holy grail of game design, and music is one of the most effective tools for achieving it. A steady, unobtrusive soundtrack can help players maintain focus and lose track of time, which is why you suddenly realize it’s 3 a.m. and you’ve been playing *Civilization* for eight hours straight. The music in these games is often ambient and non-linear, designed to loop seamlessly without becoming repetitive or distracting. It’s the auditory equivalent of a gentle current, carrying you along without you even noticing.

Then there’s “emotional tagging,” the process by which our brains associate specific sounds with specific emotions. The triumphant fanfare that plays when you level up, the jaunty tune that accompanies a successful puzzle solution, the ominous drone that signals an impending boss battle – these are all examples of emotional tagging in action. Over time, these associations become so ingrained that the music alone is enough to trigger the corresponding emotion. It’s a form of classical conditioning, and it’s incredibly effective. This is why the sound of a collecting a coin in *Super Mario Bros.* is so satisfying, and why the sound of a headshot in *Call of Duty* is so… well, let’s just say it’s also satisfying.

But perhaps the most insidious psychological trick is the “anticipation loop.” This is where music is used to create a sense of expectation, a feeling that something is about to happen. The rising tempo of the music as you approach the final boss, the subtle shift in the melody when you’re close to discovering a secret – these are all designed to keep you on the edge of your seat, eager to see what happens next. It’s a powerful tool for maintaining engagement, and it’s one that game designers use with surgical precision. The music in the *Metal Gear Solid* series is a masterclass in this, with its use of dynamic, multi-layered tracks that change in intensity depending on whether you’re sneaking around undetected or in the middle of a full-blown firefight.

And let’s not forget the role of music in shaping consumer behavior. The celebratory jingle that plays when you open a loot box, the satisfying “cha-ching” of an in-game purchase – these are all designed to create a positive feedback loop, to make you feel good about spending money. It’s a subtle but powerful form of manipulation, and it’s one that has made the free-to-play model so incredibly profitable. The music tells you that you’ve done something good, that you’ve been rewarded, and it encourages you to do it again. And again. And again. It’s the auditory equivalent of a slot machine, and it’s just as addictive.

Finally, there’s the often-overlooked power of silence. Just as a rest in a piece of music can be as powerful as a note, the absence of music in a game can be incredibly effective. The sudden silence that falls when you enter a new area, the way the music cuts out just before a jump scare – these are all ways that game designers use silence to create tension and to focus the player’s attention. In a world of constant noise, silence can be the most powerful sound of all.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The history of video games is littered with examples of iconic soundtracks that have become as famous as the games themselves. The *Super Mario Bros.* theme, for instance, is one of the most recognizable pieces of music in the world. Its cheerful, upbeat melody is the perfect accompaniment to Mario’s adventures, and it’s a masterclass in creating a positive emotional experience.

Then there’s the music of *The Legend of Zelda*. The main theme, with its soaring, heroic melody, perfectly captures the epic scale of the game’s world. And the music that plays when you enter a dungeon, with its sense of mystery and foreboding, is a brilliant example of how music can be used to create atmosphere.

But it’s not just about original compositions. The use of licensed music in games has also become increasingly popular. The *Grand Theft Auto* series, with its in-game radio stations playing a curated selection of real-world songs, is a prime example. The music not only helps to establish the game’s setting and time period, but it also allows players to personalize their experience, to create their own soundtrack to their criminal escapades.

And let’s not forget the more… manipulative examples. The music of *Candy Crush Saga*, with its saccharine melodies and celebratory sound effects, is a perfect example of how music can be used to create a sense of reward and to encourage repeated play. The game is a masterclass in the use of positive reinforcement, and the music is a key part of its addictive formula.

Finally, there’s the case of *Tetris*. The game’s iconic theme, a Russian folk song called “Korobeiniki,” is one of the most famous earworms in history. Its simple, repetitive melody is the perfect accompaniment to the game’s addictive gameplay, and it’s a testament to the power of a good tune to get stuck in your head and never, ever leave.

But the world of game music is far more diverse than these few examples might suggest. The *Final Fantasy* series, for instance, is known for its epic, orchestral scores that are as much a part of the experience as the story and the gameplay. The music of Nobuo Uematsu, the composer for many of the early games in the series, is beloved by fans around the world, and his compositions are often performed in concert halls to sold-out audiences.

Then there’s the music of *Chrono Trigger*, a game that is widely regarded as one of the greatest of all time. The soundtrack, composed by Yasunori Mitsuda and Nobuo Uematsu, is a masterpiece of musical storytelling, with each character and time period having its own distinct musical theme. The music is so integral to the experience that it’s hard to imagine the game without it.

And let’s not forget the more recent examples. The soundtrack to the 2016 reboot of *Doom* is a relentless assault of industrial metal and electronic noise that perfectly complements the game’s fast-paced, brutal gameplay. The music is so aggressive that it actually makes you a better player, encouraging you to move faster and to kill with more… enthusiasm. It’s a brilliant example of how music can be used to enhance gameplay and to create a truly visceral experience.

## DISCUSSION

The implications of the psychology of game music extend far beyond the world of video games. The same principles that are used to create immersive and engaging game experiences are also being applied in other areas, from marketing and advertising to education and therapy. The use of music to influence mood and behavior is a powerful tool, and it’s one that is being used with increasing sophistication.

In the world of marketing, for example, retailers have long known that playing slow-tempo music can encourage shoppers to stay in a store longer, while fast-paced music can lead to quicker purchasing decisions. The same is true for online shopping, where the right background music can create a more pleasant and engaging experience, and can even influence the types of products that people buy. It’s no accident that luxury brands often use classical music in their advertising, while fast-food chains opt for upbeat pop songs. The music is a subtle cue, a way of telling you how you should feel about the product.

In education, music is being used to create more engaging and effective learning environments. The use of background music has been shown to improve focus and concentration, and the use of musical jingles and songs can help students to remember information more easily. It’s a far cry from the traditional classroom, but it’s a testament to the power of music to enhance the learning process. The success of shows like *Schoolhouse Rock!* is a testament to this. Who among us doesn’t know that “conjunction junction, what’s your function?”

And in therapy, music is being used to help people with a wide range of conditions, from anxiety and depression to dementia and autism. Music therapy has been shown to be an effective tool for reducing stress, improving mood, and promoting social interaction. It’s a powerful reminder that the same music that can make us feel a sense of dread in a horror game can also be used to bring comfort and healing in the real world. The use of personalized playlists for dementia patients, for example, has been shown to be incredibly effective at evoking memories and improving cognitive function.

Of course, there are also ethical considerations to take into account. The use of music to manipulate behavior, particularly in the context of in-game purchases and gambling, raises some serious questions. Where do we draw the line between entertainment and exploitation? It’s a debate that is likely to continue for years to come, and it’s one that we, as consumers, need to be aware of. The use of “dark patterns” in game design, where psychological tricks are used to encourage players to spend money, is a growing concern. And music is often a key part of these dark patterns.

## CONCLUSION

So, the next time you find yourself humming the theme from *The Legend of Zelda* or tapping your foot to the beat of a *Donkey Kong Country* level, take a moment to appreciate the artistry and the psychology that went into its creation. That music is more than just a collection of notes; it’s a carefully crafted tool designed to transport you to another world, to make you feel a range of emotions, and to keep you coming back for more. It’s a testament to the power of sound to shape our experiences and to influence our behavior.

And as we’ve seen, the principles of game music design are not confined to the virtual world. They are being used all around us, in ways that are both beneficial and, at times, a little bit sneaky. So, the next time you find yourself inexplicably drawn to a particular product in a store, or find yourself tapping your credit card details into a website with a little too much enthusiasm, take a moment to listen to the music that’s playing in the background. You might just find that you’re being played.
references:
[1] Evans, R. (2018). An Investigation into the Effect of Music on Immersion in Video Games. *Music Science*. https://musicscience.net/wp-content/uploads/2018/10/evans.pdf

[2] Klimmt, C., Possler, D., May, N., Auge, H., Wanjek, L., & Wolf, A.-L. (2019). Effects of soundtrack music on the video game experience. *Media Psychology*, *22*(5), 689–713. https://doi.org/10.1080/15213269.2018.1507827

[3] Belfi, A. M., Karlan, B., & Tranel, D. (2016). Music Evokes Vivid Autobiographical Memories. *Memory*, *24*(7), 979–989. https://doi.org/10.1080/09658211.2015.1064018

[4] Renowned for Sound. (n.d.). The Strategies Games Employ: Does Music Have an Effect on Your Game Choices? *Renowned for Sound*. https://renownedforsound.com/the-strategies-games-employ-does-music-have-an-effect-on-your-game-choices/

[5] GameGrin. (2025, December 2). The Psychology of Game Music and Why It Keeps Players Engaged. *GameGrin*. https://www.gamegrin.com/articles/the-psychology-of-game-music-and-why-it-keeps-players-engaged/

[6] Joff Winks. (2023, December 28). Why Is Music In Video Games So Important? *Joff Winks*. https://www.joffwinks.com/blog/why-is-music-in-video-games-so-important
